title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteriodea; order: Paxillosida; family: Astropectinidae; genus: Astropecten; species: irregularis pontoporeus; common name: Astropecten orange trim

title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: Distinct marginal plates separated by grooves on aboral and oral sides. Lower marginal plates project beyond upper plates to form a distinct edge to disc and arms. Both series of marginal plates bear spines. Tube feet in two rows. Node in centre of disc sometimes raised (anal cone). Disc plates (paxillae) fine, often darker brown in colour, sometimes with distinct line down centre of each arm. Plates on upper surface with clusters of short spinelets. Madreporite in a slightly depressed area near marginal plate.

title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: Pale orange to apricot/pink marginal plates, with darker pink/brown/mauve body. Distinct darker brown/purple lines along central aboral side of each arm. Often brighter orange bands separate each marginal plate. Pale cream colouring on oral side.

title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: Up to 90 mm diameter.

title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: Common on both West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 50 m to +200 m.

title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: Astropecten antares, which has shorter, wider, more petal-shaped arms. A. irregularis pontoporeus arms taper more and are longer.

title: Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 50-51. (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 25-44 (794pp.).

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteriodea; order: Paxillosida; family: Astropectinidae; genus: Astropecten; species: cingulatus; common name: Shallow water Astropecten

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: Has relatively short, petaloid (petal-like) arms and distinct marginal plates on both aboral and oral surfaces with distinctly elongated oral marginal plates. Lower marginal plates project beyond upper plates to form a distinct edge to disc and arms. A deeper mid-line colouration can be evident on the aboral disc plates (paxillae). Both series of marginal plates bear spines. Tube feet in two rows. Sometimes node raised in centre of disc (anal cone).

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: Dusty pink to brown/purple colouring on upper surface. The spines protruding from the marginal plate may be dark purple-brown but pale towards the tips. Pale cream colouring on oral side.

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: Up to 90 mm diameter.

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: This is a shallow-water species found more commonly on the South Coast of South Africa, from 0-65 m depth.

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: Similar to Astropecten irregularis pontoporeus, but the marginal plates in A. irregularis pontoporeus are pale in comparison to A.cingulatus, which has petaloid arms and elongated oral marginal plates.

title: Astropecten cingulatus
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 33. (277pp.).

title: Astropecten exilis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Paxillosida; family: Astropectinidae; genus: Astropecten; species: exilis; common name: Long-arm Astropecten

title: Astropecten exilis
content: Small disc; long, narrow tapering arms, flexible. Fine- grained aboral (top) plates, papillae-like. Distinct marginal plates on both aboral and oral sides. Three long spines on outer edge of oral marginal plate. Two rows of tube feet ending in a point, but without sucker disc.

title: Astropecten exilis
content: Light brown in colour, marginal plates paler in colour.

title: Astropecten exilis
content: 150 mm diameter.

title: Astropecten exilis
content: Previously recorded off Natal, however trawl specimens found along West and South Coasts of South Africa. Depth from 180 m to ±250 m.

title: Astropecten exilis
content: Other Astropecten species and Cheiraster hirsutus, however A. exilis has distinctly long, strap-like arms that are fairly fragile. Spines of marginal plates usually fold flat on capture.

content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 49. (277pp.). Species confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Paxillosida; family: Astropectinidae; genus: Dipsacaster; species: sladeni capensis; common name: Coarse-grained orange star

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: A common deep-water starfish found off South Africa. Distinct, large, star-shaped body form (stellate). Arms form triangle shape with body, ranging ~70-100 mm in diameter. Relatively large disc, coarse body texture. Arms tapering and pointed. Madreporite covered over by paxillae. Paxillae in regular rows. Tube feet are pointed. Marginal plates conspicuous and slightly swollen. Ventral marginal plate (inferomarginal) projects beyond the aboral marginal plate (superomarginal), defining the edge of the body when viewed from above.

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: Bright orange to reddish orange.

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: Mostly 70-100 mm; can reach up to 150 mm diameter.

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: West Coast of South Africa to East London, from ± 110 m to 630 m depth.

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: Dipsacaster sladeni, which is a subspecies.

title: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 52-53. (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 50-51. (794pp.). Species confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Persephonaster sp.
content: Large in size (70-100 mm diameter), coarse body texture, plates at margin conspicuous and slightly swollen. Appears similar to degraded Dipsacaster sladeni capensis, but specimens are required to confirm accurate identification.

title: Persephonaster sp.
content: Pale orange to apricot colour.

title: Persephonaster sp.
content: 70-100 mm diameter.

content: South Coast of South Africa.

title: Persephonaster sp.

title: Psilaster acuminatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Paxillosida; family: Astropectinidae; genus: Psilaster; species: acuminatus; common name: Pale orange fine-grained star

title: Psilaster acuminatus
content: Leathery star with fine disc plates (paxillae), distinct marginal plates with dividing grooves. Marginal plates become more ‘rolled’ inwards towards the distal (end) part of the arms. Raised node in centre of disc (anal cone). Madreporite is evident. Long arms tapering to narrow, pointed tips. No obvious projecting spines visible to the naked eye. The tube feet are pointed and occur in two rows.

title: Psilaster acuminatus
content: Pale orange to dark pink.

title: Psilaster acuminatus
content: Up to 180 mm diameter across arms. Smaller individuals 40-50 mm width.

title: Psilaster acuminatus
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa, 155-550 m or deeper.

title: Psilaster acuminatus
content: None.

title: Plutonaster cf. intermedius
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Paxillosida; family: Astropectinidae; genus: Plutonaster; species: cf. intermedius; common name: Intermediate starfish

title: Plutonaster cf. intermedius
content: Arms moderate length, narrow, tapering more abruptly in the basal part than beyond, tips blunt. Terminal plates more or less truncated (cut short); paxillae (plates) with low rounded columns crowned with 12-30 short spinelets, which emerge directly from the marginal plate. Madreporite covered with paxillae. Stiff, inflexible starfish. Specimens seldom encountered in trawls and are needed for confirming identification.

title: Plutonaster cf. intermedius
content: Pale orange with white marginal plates.

title: Plutonaster cf. intermedius
content: Average ± 80 mm diameter, but larger up to 150 mm diameter have been recorded.

title: Plutonaster cf. intermedius
content: Occurs on West and South Coasts of South Africa, around 350 m depth.

title: Plutonaster cf. intermedius
content: Persephonaster sp. and Dipsacaster sladeni capensis. Other species of Plutonaster spp. may occur in the region and may have distinct spines on the inferomarginal plates.

content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 66-73 (794pp.).

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Notomyotida; family: Benthopectinidae; genus: Cheiraster; species: hirsutus; common name: Spiky orange centre star

title: Cheiraster hirsutus
content: Tips of arms often curled at ends. Numerous spines, both small and larger, protrude from aboral marginal edge. Long, thin, tapering arms. Double rows of tube feet. Single aboral spine shorter than oral (underside) spines. Two oral (underside) spines, one nearly twice the length of the other.

title: Cheiraster hirsutus
content: Ranging from light to dark pink and pale to bright orange.

title: Cheiraster hirsutus
content: Up to 110 mm diameter. Disc 20 mm diameter.

title: Cheiraster hirsutus
content: Predominantly West Coast region of South Africa.

title: Cheiraster hirsutus
content: Can appear similar to some Astropecten species, however Cheiraster hirsutus is distinct in having particularly long spines, suckered tube feet and tips of arms curl up on capture.

title: Cheiraster hirsutus
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 126-136 (794pp.).

title: Stegnobrisinga splendens
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Brisingida; family: Brisingidae; genus: Stegnobrisinga; species: splendens; common name: Brisingid rigid

title: Stegnobrisinga splendens
content: Characterised by having a small, circular disc, sharply differentiated from long, slender, rigid and usually deciduous arms (arms fall off), always more than five, usually between 11 to 14. Tube feet suckered in two rows. More rigid, calcified skeleton with raised, ridged markings (furrows) along arms.

title: Stegnobrisinga splendens
content: Orange, with white ridges.

title: Stegnobrisinga splendens
content: Arms up to 200 mm long, disc up to 30 mm diameter.

title: Stegnobrisinga splendens
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa. Deep-water species 800-4 000 m.

title: Stegnobrisinga splendens
content: Coronaster volsellatus, but Stegnobrisinga splendens is more rigid and calcified and has raised, ridged markings traversing arms. Brisinga cricophora also occurs in the region and appears very similar to S. splendens. Microscopic examination required to distinguish.

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Spinulosida; family: Echinasteridae; genus: Henricia; species: abyssalis; common name: Apricot puffy-arm star

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: Small disc; long, tapering, ‘puffy’ arms. Whitened arm tips that often curl in at ends. Arms and disc inflated (puffy). Small papillae cover entire disc and arms. Aboral surface appears covered in very fine mesh work. Madreporite located midway between centre and arm edge. Two rows of tube feet.

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: Pale yellow, pale orange, apricot or bright orange.

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: Average 80 mm diameter; up to 175 mm diameter.

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa, 56-408 m.

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: Henricia ornata, but H. abyssalis more common and distinguished by the white tips.

title: Henricia abyssalis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 88. (277pp.).

title: Henricia ornata
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Spinulosida; family: Echinasteridae; genus: Henricia; species: ornata; common name: Reticulated star

title: Henricia ornata
content: Appears similar to Henricia abyssalis, however surface texture is described as irregular-honeycombed. Arms long and tapering, with small disc. Arms and disc inflated (puffy). Two rows of tube feet.

title: Henricia ornata
content: Orange to maroon.

title: Henricia ornata
content: Up to 100 mm diameter.

title: Henricia ornata
content: Occurs predominantly on South Coast, South Africa. Intertidal to 90 m.

title: Henricia ornata
content: Henricia abyssalis, but H. ornata has spotted appearance (irregular-honeycombed) on aboral surface and usually deeper/darker colour.

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Gilbertaster; species: anacanthus; common name: Gilbert’s star

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: Arms long and narrow, tapering abruptly at the base and then very slightly throughout to the blunt tip. Disc is of fair size and central part of arms often inflated. Marginal plates are well rounded in shape. No spines of any description occur on general body surface. Very large (1.5 mm), bivalved pedicellaria (claw-shaped structure) present on aboral and oral surfaces, but not on marginal plates. Each marginal plate is covered with close-set, superficially flat, large, irregular granules. Granules around the border of the plate are smaller and form in irregular patterns.

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: Orange to red.

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: 165 mm diameter and bigger.

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: One specimen collected from South Coast, South Africa (2014) at 638 m. This species is known primarily from the tropical North Pacific (Hawaiian Islands area).

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: Similar in shape to Mediaster bairdi capensis, but Gilbertaster anacanthus have large, obvious pedicellaria covering aboral and oral surfaces.

title: Gilbertaster anacanthus
content: Fisher, WK. 1906. The starfishes of the Hawaiian Islands. Bulletin of the United States Fish Commission 23: 987-1130. p. 1045. Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Calliaster; species: acanthodes; common name: Spiky sheriff star

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: Long, sharp and very distinct marginal spines along outer edges, with smaller spines covering the aboral surface. Distinct marginal plates separated by grooves, with long spines emerging from each aboral and oral plate. Pentagon-shaped central disc, but with elongated arms. Six to nine slender furrow spines. Strong, sharp spines on the marginal edges.

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: Orange, with brown markings on central disc.

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: Up to ± 120 mm in diameter.

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: South African endemic. South to East Coasts of South Africa. Not usually found on West Coast. Occur at depths between ~ 130 and 420 m.

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: Calliaster baccatus, which has three to four furrow spines, blunt spines on surface and no sharp spines on marginal plates; and Hippasteria phyrangiana, which has blunt, stout marginal spines and bivalve pedicilaria.

title: Calliaster acanthodes
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 60-61. (277pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Calliaster; species: baccatus; common name: Blunt sheriff star

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: Pentagon-shaped central disc, with elongated arms ending in bluntly rounded tips. Marginal plates square shaped and conspicuous. Calliaster baccatus has three to four furrow spines on plates lining the tube feet grooves. Blunt, bullet-shaped spines on the marginal edges and aboral surface (but no sharp spines present). Pedicellariae are rare or absent.

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: Orange, brick red to brown colouration and frequently mottled in colour.

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: Up to ± 100 mm in diameter.

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: South African endemic. South to East Coasts of South Africa. Not usually found on West Coast. Occur at depths between ~ 10 and 23 m.

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: Calliaster acanthodes (has sharper pointed spines along marginal plates and aboral surface) and Hippasteria phyrangia (blunt, stout marginal spines and obvious bivalve pedicellariae).

title: Calliaster baccatus
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 61. (277pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Ceramaster; species: patagonicus euryplax; common name: Shiny red sheriff star

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: Well-defined marginal plates separated by grooves. Rigid body with slightly inflated areas over the midradial ridge. Pentagon-shaped with short, webbed arms. Double rows of tube feet. Tips of each arm with a white plate. Often smooth and shiny aboral surface.

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: Bright red to orange, with pale tips at end of each arm. Pale white to yellow oral surface.

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: Up to 70 mm diameter.

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, 150-462 m.

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: Toraster tuberculatus and Odontaster australis, but C. granularis is usually a bright, shiny red with a smoother aboral texture.

title: Ceramaster patagonicus euryplax
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 61-62 (277pp.).

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Cladaster; species: macrobrachius; common name: Macro-clad starfish

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: Stellate-shaped with well-developed, pronounced arms tapering to rounded tips. Two rows of tube feet. Marginal plates, square in shape, are covered by widely spaced, coarse granules. In preservation, these granules rub off readily and leave pits. Body is well calcified, i.e. quite rigid. Broad-valved pedicellaria (claw-shape structure) clearly visible on oral surface.

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: Pale orange, with white areas and white pedicellaria on aboral, becoming paler to white towards edges and tips of arms.

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: ± 60 mm diameter.

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: Southern African endemic. Recorded on West and South Coasts of South Africa, but rarely encountered. Depth recorded from 420 to 914 m.

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: Gilbertaster anacanthus, which has large pedicellaria on both aboral and oral surfaces; Mediaster bairdi capensis, which do not have large pedicellaria evident.

title: Cladaster macrobrachius
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 62. (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 239-240. (794pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Hippasteria; species: phrygiana; common name: Thorny starfish

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: A pentagonal-shaped starfish with fairly short, less pronounced arms. Marginal plates are large, smooth and conspicuous in aboral view and have one or two pronounced, stout spines emerging from each marginal plate. There are no spines on the aboral surface, which has a coarsely granulated appearance. On the oral surface large, obvious clam-shaped pedicellaria are present.

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: Brick red to orange.

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: Up to 260 mm diameter, but small individuals likely to occur.

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: Mostly occur on South Coast of South Africa, from 310 to 980 m.

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: Toraster tuberculatus.

title: Hippasteria phrygiana
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 63 (277pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Hippasteria; species: falklandica; common name: Falkland starfish

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: A pentagonal-shaped starfish with fairly pronounced arms. Marginal plates are large; smooth granules which are conspicuous in aboral view but do not have marginal spines. There are no spines on the aboral surface, which has a coarsely granulated appearance. On the oral and aboral surface large, obvious, clam-shaped pedicellaria are present.

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: Orange.

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: Up to 130 mm diameter recorded, but small individuals likely to occur.

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: Mostly occurring on South Coast of South Africa. Known from depths of 149-1 148 m.

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: Hippasteria phrygiana, but H. falklandica does not have marginal spines; Toraster tuberculatus which have large, bald, convex tubercles covering the oral surface.

title: Hippasteria falklandica
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. p. 247 (794pp.). Mah C, Neill K, Eléaume M and Foltz D. 2014. title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Mediaster; species: bairdi capensis; common name: Orange sheriff star

title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: Commonly occurring inflexible, rigid star with broad disc. Marginal plates distinct, block-shaped and covered with granules, separated by grooves on upper surface. Tube feet end in a blunt sucker tip. Disc plates distinct and large, with distinct checkerboard appearance. Arms taper narrowly and immediately.

title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: Orange to red.

title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: Average up to 70 mm diameter.

title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa.

title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: Dipsacaster sladeni capensis, Gilbertaster anacanthus, Odontaster sp. body slightly more flexible and webbing between arms not as pronounced. Arm tips curl upwards at times. Easily confused with Odontaster australis, but M. bairdi capensis has more distinct marginal plates and does not have enlarged tooth surrounding mouth opening.

title: Mediaster bairdi capensis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 64. (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 251-253 (794pp.).

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Goniasteridae; genus: Toraster; species: tuberculatus; common name: Red sheriff star Inflated state

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: Commonly occurring, rigid starfish with broad disc and short arms. Pentagonal to stellate in body shape. Distinct bald tubercles cover the entire aboral surface. Marginal plates distinct, granulated and separated by grooves on upper surface. Distinct madreporite. Distal plates (towards arm tips) often swollen or enlarged. Arm tips vary from either sharply pointed to bluntly rounded. Abactinal plates larger in size along radial lines. Ventral plates covered with granules. Body of starfish sometimes inflated when landed from a trawl net, but deflates over time.

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: Red, brown, dark orange on aboral; pale cream to yellow on oral side.

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: Up to 160 mm diameter.

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa. Has been reported from Durban area.

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: Ceramaster granularis, Odontaster australis, Hippasteria phrygiana.

title: Toraster tuberculatus
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. p. 267 (794pp.). Mortensen T. 1933. Echinoderms of South Africa (Asteroidea and Ophiuroidea): Papers from Dr Th. Mortensens’s Pacific Expedition 1914–1916, Videnskabelige Meddelelser fra Dansk Naturhistorisk Forening. 93: 215-400. Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Paxillosida; family: Luidiidae; genus: Luidia; species: sarsii africana; common name: Legs break easily starfish

title: Luidia sarsii africana
content: Arms usually break off central disc very easily. Distinct spines protrude from aboral margin edge; arms long, flexible, flattened and tapering, strap-like. Usually five arms.

title: Luidia sarsii africana
content: Brown to dark pink.

title: Luidia sarsii africana
content: Average up to 150 mm diameter, but can get larger individuals.

title: Luidia sarsii africana
content: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, to Port Elizabeth; 54 m to 360+ m depth.

title: Luidia sarsii africana
content: Astropecten polyacanthus and Astropecten exilis, however arms of Luidia africana are more flattened and broader, i.e. less tapered, and break off central disc easily.

title: Luidia sarsii africana
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. p. 20. (794pp.).

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Poraniidae; genus: Chondraster; species: elattosis; common name: Pentagon star

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: Inflexible, rigid star with thick, solid, spongy disc. Pentagonal in shape. Marginal plates indistinct. Distinct madreporite. Fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) form distinct rows along each arm, but no spines apparent. Thick fleshy starfish with smooth aboral and oral surface. Double rows of tube feet. No marginal plates visible. Patterning on aboral surface can be very distinct when brooding (see third image).

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: Bright pink to orange on aboral; pale yellow on oral surface.

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: Can reach up to 230 mm diameter.

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: South African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 400 to 1 000+ m depth.

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: Spoladaster veneris, but Chondraster ellatosis does not inflate and is more leathery.

title: Chondraster elattosis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 73-74 (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 202-204 (794pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Poraniidae; genus: Spoladaster; species: veneris; common name: Inflated star

title: Spoladaster veneris
content: Pentagonal in shape, cushion-like body, often inflated when landed (as in photo), but slowly deflates with time out of water. Numerous papillae coat the aboral surface. Ventral smooth with fine lines.

title: Spoladaster veneris
content: Speckled brilliant orange aboral surface and pale cream smooth oral surface.

title: Spoladaster veneris
content: Up to 160 mm diameter.

title: Spoladaster veneris
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 40 to 205+ m depth.

title: Spoladaster veneris
content: Chondraster elattosis, but S. brachyactis inflates and is not as leathery.

title: Spoladaster veneris
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 222-224 (794pp.).

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteriodea; order: Valvatida; family: Poraniidae; genus: Poraniopsis; species: echinaster; common name: Spiky cushion star

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: Short-armed, stellate body form with a reticular skeleton (spiky skeleton with soft tissue covering). Distinct raised spines covering the aboral surface 1-4 mm in length. Arms fairly rigid, with ends often turning upwards or curling inwards. Two rows of tube feet. Madreporite white in colour, located off- centre halfway to base of arms. Strong spines along the base of arms.

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: Deep orange to red or even pure white, with spines light red to yellowish white. Pale oral surface.

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: Average 50 up to 160 mm diameter, mostly small specimens but occasionally large too.

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: South Atlantic including West Coast of South Africa.

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: Lophaster quadrispinus, which has many dense raised tubercles on the aboral surface or Diplopteraster multipes, which is more cushion-like, with arms that are not as clearly defined as P. echinaster.

title: Poraniopsis echinaster
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. p. 90 (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteriodea; order: Paxillosida; family: Pseudarchasteridae; genus: Pseudarchaster; species: tessellatus; common name: Dusky pink long-armed star

title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: Inflexible star with broad disc and long, tapering, rigid arms. Disc plates distinct, regular oval/circular in shape. Fine texture on aboral plates, but plates begin to separate once out of water. Distinct marginal plates on both aboral and oral sides. Two rows of tube feet mostly hidden by fine clusters of spines on the inside oral margin of each arm. Madreporite midway between disc centre and marginal plate.

title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: Dusky pink to white.

title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: Average 70 mm diameter, but up to 160 mm.

title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa.

title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: Pseudarchaster brachyactis, but P. tessellatus has longer, more tapering arms.

title: Pseudarchaster tessellatus
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 260-264 (794pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteriodea; order: Paxillosida; family: Pseudarchasteridae; genus: Pseudarchaster; species: brachyactis; common name: Dusky pink short-armed star

title: Pseudarchaster brachyactis
content: Inflexible star with broad disc similar to Pseudarchaster tessellatus, but has shorter, stubbier arms. Fine texture on aboral plates, but plates begin to separate once out of water. Disc plates distinct. Distinct marginal plates. Two rows of tube feet.

title: Pseudarchaster brachyactis
content: Dusky pink to white.

title: Pseudarchaster brachyactis
content: Average 70 mm diameter.

title: Pseudarchaster brachyactis
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa.

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteriodea; order: Velatida; family: Pterasteridae; genus: Diplopteraster; species: multipes; common name: Large prickly slime cushion star

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: Large, fleshy and inflated disc with cover of skin supported by spines. Tips of arms appear upturned and white. Flesh ‘decomposes’ rapidly when on deck, resulting in mushy texture and production of a lot of mucus. Best to keep specimens in dish of water until ready to discard. Four rows of tube feet visible in wide tube foot grooves.

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: Pale orange, bright orange to red.

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: Up to 200-260 mm diameter.

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: Throughout West and South Coast region of South Africa.

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: Pteraster capensis can appear similar, however Diplopteraster multiples rapidly disintegrates when out of water on deck and becomes mushy very quickly, while P. capensis is firm in texture and remains so on deck.

title: Diplopteraster multipes
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 310-313 (794pp.).

title: Pteraster capensis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Velatida; family: Pterasteridae; genus: Pteraster; species: capensis; common name: Common/Brooding cushion star

title: Pteraster capensis
content: Small, puffy cushion starfish with fairly solid texture. Produce a lot of mucus when disturbed (also called Slime Stars). Plates appear as fine rosettes of holes covering aboral surface. Ends of arms turned upwards and have white tips. Specimens range in size from very tiny (20 mm diameter) to very large (150 mm diameter).

title: Pteraster capensis
content: Wide range of colours – pink, yellow, orange, brown, mottled. In deeper waters usually white, but colour variation of orange occurs on South Coast.

title: Pteraster capensis
content: Average 20-25 mm; can be larger up to 135+ mm diameter.

title: Pteraster capensis
content: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa.

title: Pteraster capensis
content: Pteraster affinus, which has more tapering arms, otherwise similar (keep a look out).

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Echinasteridae; genus: Lophaster; species: quadrispinus; common name: Four-spined starfish

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: Many raised tubercles (paxillae) covering entire aboral surface in symmetric pattern. Fairly rigid star and arms usually bent stiffly when on deck. Marginal edge with extended paxillae distinct and small tufts on tips.

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: Pale to bright orange to red.

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: Average 50 mm diameter, but larger specimens can occur.

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa.

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: Poraniopsis echinaster, but Lophaster quadrispinus does not have as spiky aboral texture and has more tubercles on aboral surface.

title: Lophaster quadrispinus
content: Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 299-301. (794pp.). Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

title: Crossaster penicillatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Solasteridae; genus: Crossaster; species: penicillatus; common name: Raspberry star/Blomme

title: Crossaster penicillatus
content: Wide flattened disc with 9 to 12 arms. Bundles of spines on aboral surface. Soft-bodied starfish with flexible spines. Very common starfish occurring in dense patches and hundreds are often landed in trawls.

title: Crossaster penicillatus
content: Orange-pink, white-pink, dark pink.

title: Crossaster penicillatus
content: Average 70 mm diameter; up to 120 mm diameter.

title: Crossaster penicillatus
content: Throughout West and South Coast region of South Africa.

title: Solaster spp.
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Solasteridae; genus: Solaster; species: spp.; common name: Sun-shaped orange star

title: Solaster spp.
content: Thick puffy arms, tapering gently to points. Small tubercles covering aboral surface (paxillae). Up to eight arms. Seldom occurs in South African waters.

title: Solaster spp.
content: Orange.

title: Solaster spp.
content: 150-200 mm diameter.

title: Solaster spp.
content: West Coast of South Africa.

title: Solaster spp.
content: Crossaster penicillatus, which is a smaller, less puffy starfish and is very abundant.

title: Odontaster australis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Asteroidea; order: Valvatida; family: Odontasteridae; genus: Odontaster; species: australis; common name: False sheriff star

title: Odontaster australis
content: Fairly rigid star with distinct marginal plates and slightly inflated disc and arms. Madreporite located off-centre, as a clearly distinguishable light spot. Wider marginal plates distinct; oral surface plates have spinules (rather than granules, as in Mediaster); fewer spines and distinct plates surrounding mouth opening. Odontaster spp. have 5 x single, long, sharply tapered teeth visible on oral surface surrounding the mouth opening (see photo) = distinguishing feature between Odontaster spp. and Mediaster spp.

title: Odontaster australis
content: Ranging from pale yellow to orange to red.

title: Odontaster australis
content: Average 70-80 mm diameter.

title: Odontaster australis
content: Southern African endemic. Known from 320 m Saldanha Bay, West Coast of South Africa. Rarely encountered in trawl surveys.

title: Odontaster australis
content: Toraster sp. and Ceramaster sp., but Odontaster sp. body slightly more flexible and webbing between arms not as pronounced. Arm tips curl upwards at times.

title: Odontaster australis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. British Museum (Natural History): London. pp. 58-59. (277pp.). Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. pp. 154-155. (794pp.).

title: Comanthus wahlbergii
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Crinoidea; order: Comatulida; family: Comasteridae; genus: Comanthus; species: wahlbergii; common name: Common feather star/Crinoid

title: Comanthus wahlbergii
content: Between 10 and 22 segmented arms that originate from a small, central disc, below which are cirri which attach the animal to the seafloor or rock. Arms have a feather-like appearance with side branches or pinnules.

title: Comanthus wahlbergii
content: White, pink, orange to pale brown or yellow, often variegated.

title: Comanthus wahlbergii
content: Arms can be up to 150 mm in length.

title: Comanthus wahlbergii
content: South-western Cape, South Coast and southern reach of East Coast of South Africa. Shallow to ± 60 m and possibly deeper.

title: Comanthus wahlbergii
content: Sea lilies, which are distinguished from feather stars (Comanthus wahlbergii) by the absence of a stalk in feather stars. Tropiometra carinata is a similar species, but usually smaller and have finer, more numerous pinnules and only 10 long arms.

title: Goniocidaris indica
content: Robust, small urchin. Sturdy, thorny primary spines with umbrella-like structures at base. Spines readily detach from the test.

title: Goniocidaris indica
content: Pinkish-cream test, with brownish spines.

title: Goniocidaris indica
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 25 mm.

title: Goniocidaris indica
content: South Coast of South Africa, Maldives, Tanzania; 160-620 m depth range.

title: Goniocidaris indica
content: None. Umbrella-like structures distinguish Goniocidaris indica.

title: Stereocidaris excavata
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Cidaroida; family: Cidaridae; genus: Stereocidaris; species: excavata; common name: Pencil urchin

title: Stereocidaris excavata
content: Large, robust urchin. Sturdy, long, slender, serrated, flute-like primary spines (although often easily detach from test). Darkened secondary spines encircling base of primary spines. Dark, double rows of miliary spines, extending from top to bottom of test (ambulacrum). Anal area, sunken with centrally positioned, elevated pores.

title: Stereocidaris excavata
content: Beige to brown, with darkened secondary spines at base of primary spine and darkened ambulacrum. May have a green tint.

title: Stereocidaris excavata
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 69 mm.

title: Stereocidaris excavata
content: Endemic to the South Coast of South Africa; 120-170 m depth range.

title: Stereocidaris excavata
content: Stereocidaris capensis, which is smaller (up to 36 mm diameter). S. capensis lacks darkened secondary spines at the base of the primary spine.

title: Stereocidaris excavata

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Cidaroida; family: Histocidaridae; genus: Histocidaris; species: purpurata; common name: Purple pencil urchin

title: Histocidaris purpurata
content: Round, robust test. Long, robust, pointy primary spines with darkened smooth base and lighter ridged extensions. Secondary spines considerably shorter, flattened, narrowing to a blunt tip.

title: Histocidaris purpurata
content: Brown underlying test and brown to red secondary spines. Base of primary spines deep purplish-red, with contrasting pale pink to white at tips.

title: Histocidaris purpurata
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 28 mm.

title: Histocidaris purpurata
content: South Coast of South Africa, and globally North Atlantic, Indian Ocean and New Zealand; 750-1080 m depth range.

title: Histocidaris purpurata
content: Coelopleurus spp. have similar red to pink colouring, but spines are banded.

title: Hygrosoma petersii
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Echinothurioida; family: Echinothuriidae; genus: Hygrosoma; species: petersii; common name: Grey Tam O'Shanter

title: Hygrosoma petersii
content: Test circular, collapsed. Large tubercles (structures bearing spines) and distinctive areoles (circular outlines around tubercles). Spines bearing poisonous glands (handle with caution). Believed to serve as a host to juvenile cusk eels.

title: Hygrosoma petersii
content: Light grey/green in colour, sometimes dark violet.

title: Hygrosoma petersii
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 180 mm.

title: Hygrosoma petersii
content: West and South Coasts of South Africa, Atlantic; 200-3 200 m depth range.

title: Hygrosoma petersii
content: Several Echinothuriidae species occur in the region, distinguished from these by tubercle arrangement, where tubercles disappear towards mouth (peristome) in H. petersii.

title: Hygrosoma petersii

title: Phormosoma placenta africana
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Echinothurioida; family: Echinothuriidae; genus: Phormosoma; species: placenta africana; common name: Beret urchin/Tam O'Shanter

title: Phormosoma placenta africana
content: Soft, flexible, disc-shaped test, texture leather- like, usually collapsed in trawl. Deepened areoles (circular areas around spine-bearing structure). Short, uniform spines, easily brushed off. Spines bearing poisonous glands (handle with caution).

title: Phormosoma placenta africana
content: Usually dark purple, but may also occur in other colours.

title: Phormosoma placenta africana
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 120 mm.

title: Phormosoma placenta africana
content: Endemic to the West Coast of South Africa; at 50-3 700 m depth range.

title: Phormosoma placenta africana
content: Hygrosoma petersii, but P. placenta africana differs in that both large tubercles (structure bearing spines) and areoles disappear towards mouth (peristome).

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Camarodonta; family: Echinidae; genus: Dermechinus; species: horridus africanus; common name: Orange pumpkin urchin

title: Dermechinus horridus africanus
content: Globular, delicate and extremely high test (pumpkin- like appearance), becoming more vertically raised with age. Slender, fragile, sparsely arranged spines that readily detach from test. Primary spines longer than secondary ones. Distinct white tubercles in rows from oral to aboral sides.

title: Dermechinus horridus africanus
content: Bright, sometimes pale, orange to red.

title: Dermechinus horridus africanus
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 90 mm; maximum height 120 mm.

title: Dermechinus horridus africanus
content: West and South Coast region of South Africa, Pacific and Antarctica; 30-1 020 m depth range.

title: Dermechinus horridus africanus
content: Apart from the subspecies (Dermechinus horridus horridus), other similar species known thus far is Pseudechinus marionus from Marion Island.

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Camarodonta; family: Echinidae; genus: Echinus; species: gilchristi; common name: Spiky/common sea urchin

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: Round test, dorsally compressed and wider laterally (short, squat). Thin, hollow, brittle spines readily broken in trawl net. Mouth with protruding teeth and fleshy lip around opening.

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: Test brownish to pink and sometimes greenish, primary spines uniform white, green or pale pink, secondary spines red-brownish, sometimes greenish. Distinct darker bands in double rows running from dorsal to ventral side.

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 84 mm.

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: Endemic to the West and South Coast region of South Africa; at 40-500 m depth range.

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: Polyechinus agulhensis, which lacks fleshy tissue around mouth.

title: Echinus gilchristi
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The echinoderms of southern Africa. Publ. No. 766. British Museum (Nat. Hist), London. pp. 277. (277pp.). Filander Z and Griffiths C. 2017. content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Camarodonta; family: Echinidae; genus: Polyechinus; species: agulhensis; common name: Large spiky urchin

title: Polyechinus agulhensis
content: Conically shaped test, sloping upwards (volcano- shaped), although this shape is often only evident in large specimens. Smaller specimens have similar shape to Echinus gilchristi. Stout but brittle, long primary spines; secondary spines shorter.

title: Polyechinus agulhensis
content: Variable colour – pink, green, white, purple. Distinct darker bands in double rows running from dorsal to ventral side.

title: Polyechinus agulhensis
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 86 mm wide, 58 mm high.

title: Polyechinus agulhensis
content: Endemic to the West and South Coast region of South Africa; at 200-1 080 m depth range.

title: Polyechinus agulhensis
content: Echinus gilchristi, but P. agulhensis has a more tapered, sloping test in volcano shape and lacks fleshy ring around mouth.

title: Clypeaster eurychorius
content: Live animal yellow to green.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Spatangoida; family: Brissidae; genus: Brissopsis; species: lyrifera capensis; common name: Brissopsis/heart urchin

title: Brissopsis lyrifera capensis
content: Elongated, heart-shaped test, with distinct frontal notch. Petals straight, divergent, anterior ones longer than posterior. Thin, short, fragile uniform spines, generally fall off in trawl net. Some specimens with distinct darker brown/black fasciole in shape of lyre on dorsal surface, but not all individuals have this marking.

title: Brissopsis lyrifera capensis
content: Brown, with some individuals (but not all) having a distinct darker line in shape of lyre.

title: Brissopsis lyrifera capensis
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 70 mm.

title: Brissopsis lyrifera capensis
content: Endemic to the West and South Coast region of South Africa; 5-1 400 m.

title: Echinocardium cordatum
content: Moderately high, oval-shaped test, with deepened anterior notch, frequently with a red colouration. Distinctive, wide petals, with conspicuous pores containing tube feet. Anterior petals longer than posterior ones. Spines closely packed, directed backwards.

title: Echinocardium cordatum
content: White to pale beige/cream, sometimes with red colouration around the frontal notch.

content: Maximum horizontal diameter 90 mm.

title: Echinocardium cordatum
content: Cosmopolitan species, reported along the entire coast of South Africa; from littoral to 230 m.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Echinoidea; order: Spatangoida; family: Spatangidae; genus: Spatangus; species: capensis; common name: Purple heart urchin

title: Spatangus capensis
content: Large urchin, deep purple in colour. Test with anterior notch, giving a heart-shaped appearance. Narrow, distinctive paired petals. Short, dense spines.

title: Spatangus capensis
content: Purple, sometimes brownish-beige, cleaned test white.

title: Spatangus capensis
content: Maximum horizontal diameter 125 mm.

title: Spatangus capensis
content: Endemic to the South and West Coasts of South Africa; 37-500 m depth range.

title: Ophiocreas spp.
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Euryalida; family: Asteroschematidae; genus: Ophiocreas; species: spp.; common name: Brown-skinned snake star

title: Ophiocreas spp.
content: Moderate in size, often attached onto other marine life when landed on deck. Arms do not branch but curl considerably, thick at bases and most of arms, thin at arm tips. Whole animal covered in thin skin, which easily tears off when damaged.

title: Ophiocreas spp.
content: Light brown, becoming darker towards arm tips. White beneath skin.

title: Ophiocreas spp.
content: Disc diameter up to 30 mm. Arms very long, but tightly curled.

title: Ophiocreas spp.
content: Unknown. Only two specimens encountered to date. Further specimens and taxonomy required.

title: Ophiocreas spp.
content: None.

title: Astrothorax waitei
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Euryalida; family: Gorgonocephalidae; genus: Astrothorax; species: waitei; common name: Apricot basket star

title: Astrothorax waitei
content: Small size, often attached onto other marine life (sea fans or sponges) when landed on deck. Disc swollen (tumid), dorsal surface and arms banded. Both dorsal and ventral sides covered in coarse and fine tubercles intermixed, ventral tubercles abruptly finer. Jaws also covered by fine tubercles. Arms five, long, do not branch, but may be tightly coiled dorso-ventrally. Arm spines, up to ten, with shape changing from thorny-tipped stumps proximally to F-shaped hooks distally.

title: Astrothorax waitei
content: Pale orange, apricot.

title: Astrothorax waitei
content: Considerably smaller than other basket stars, disc diameter up to 20 mm.

title: Astrothorax waitei
content: West Coast of South Africa to East Coast, Durban; 0-1 005 m depth.

title: Astrothorax waitei
content: None.

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Euryalida; family: Gorgonocephalidae; genus: Astrocladus; species: euryale; common name: Black and white basket star

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: Disc round, smooth. Radial shields armed with moderate to large round tubercles, which continue down arms but are absent at arm tips. Arms branch at disc margin. Arms readily detach and a tangled mass of arms may be the only parts retained. Ventral disc smooth and naked, including jaws and oral area. Oral papillae spiniform, fringe oral area including distal notches. Arm spines on ventral side of arms, conical, becoming hook-shaped towards arm tips.

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: Mainly black and white and/or grey with black surrounding tubercles on disc and arms, disc colour sometimes olive green.

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: Disc diameter up to 75 mm.

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: Endemic. West Coast, off Cape Town to East Coast, central KwaZulu-Natal; 11-555 m depth.

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: Astrodendum capensis, which is purple to pink in colour, with tubercles that do not extend down arms.

title: Astrocladus euryale
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). p. 131. (277pp.). Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. PhD dissertation, Department of Biological Sciences, University of Cape Town, South Africa. pp. 143-144. (434pp.).

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Euryalida; family: Gorgonocephalidae; genus: Astrodendrum; species: capensis; common name: Purple basket star

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: Disc round, few scattered tubercles in between radial shields. Radial shields armed with small to moderate tubercles, which are fat at their bases but pointed at their tips. Tubercles do not continue down arms. Arms branch extensively from disc margin. Ventral disc smooth and naked, sometimes with small tubercles. Oral papillae spiniform, fringe oral area excluding in distal notches.

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: Purple or reddish, may have a few white speckles on main area of disc.

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: Disc diameter up to 95 mm.

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: Southern African endemic. West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Kosi Bay, South Africa. Depth range 161-420 m.

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: Gorgonocephalus chilensis and Astrocladus euryale. Tubercles are wide at base in comparison to G. chilensis and Astrodendrum capensis is purple to red in colour.

title: Astrodendrum capensis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). p. 132. (277pp.). Olbers JM. 2016. title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Euryalida; family: Gorgonocephalidae; genus: Gorgonocephalus; species: chilensis; common name: red/chilean basket star

title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: Disc slightly inflated; dorsal areas between radial shields slightly indented. Radial shields conspicuous, narrow, densely covered in conical tubercles; remainder of disc covered in skin with numerous scattered tubercles, sometimes smaller in size. Disc margin with few larger tubercles. Ventral interradial areas covered in skin with small, scattered, low tubercles, few scattered tubercles towards oral area. Five arms, branching from or within disc. Arms readily detach and tangled mass of arms may be the only parts retained. Oral papillae and teeth spiniform, fringe oral frame, but absent in distal notches.

title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: Brick red, pink to light brown in colour, with white speckles.

title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: Up to 64 mm disc diameter.

title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: West Coast, off Cape Town to East Coast, Port Edward; 22-900 m depth.

title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum and Astrodendrum capensis, but G. chilensis has more tubercles on radial shields and is red or pink in colour.

title: Gorgonocephalus chilensis
content: Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. PhD dissertation, Department of Biological Sciences, University of Cape Town, South Africa. pp. 151-152. (434pp.).

title: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Euryalida; family: Gorgonocephalidae; genus: Gorgonocephalus; species: pustulatum; common name: Brown basket star

title: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum
content: Dorsal disc covering variable, sometimes naked interradially, while others with many tubercles, conical or almost spine-like. Radial shields narrow, with irregular tubercles. Ventral surface flat, covered in tubercles or may be naked. Oral papillae and teeth slender, spiniform, forming continuous fringe, but not within distal notches. Arms, five, branching from or within disc. Arms readily detach and tangled mass of arms may be the only parts retained.

title: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum
content: Brown to pink-brown with white speckles. Centre of disc dark.

title: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum
content: Up to 54 mm disc diameter.

title: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum
content: West Coast of South Africa to beyond East London; 78-860 m depth.

title: Gorgonocephalus pustulatum
content: Gorgonocephalus chilensis and Astrodendrum capensis. G. pustulatum has fewer tubercles on radial shields and is usually darker in the centre.

title: Cryptopelta aster
common_name: red and white banded brittle star
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiodermatidae; genus: Cryptopelta; species: aster; common name: red and white banded brittle star

title: Cryptopelta aster
common_name: red and white banded brittle star
content: Distinct red-and-white-banded arms with a red floret-patterned (flower-patterned) central disc. Disc pentagonal, flat, covered both dorsally and ventrally in fine granules extending onto first few arm segments. Arm spines up to seven, sometimes eight, less than half segment length.

title: Cryptopelta aster
common_name: red and white banded brittle star
content: Floret pattern red to orange and white, arms banded.

title: Cryptopelta aster
common_name: red and white banded brittle star
content: Disc diameter up to 13 mm. Arms relatively short, three times disc diameter in length.

title: Cryptopelta aster
common_name: red and white banded brittle star
content: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, reaching to East Coast, north of Durban; 75-421 m depth.

title: Cryptopelta aster
common_name: red and white banded brittle star
content: None. Distinctive red-and-white-banded arms make this species unmistakeable.

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiotrichidae; genus: Ophiothrix; species: aristulata; common name: Feathery brittle star

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: Disc round or pentagonal, disc scales on the central disc are more or less obscured by spines, spinelets or thorny stumps. Radial shields triangular, large and naked. Arms are mainly horizontally flexible (side-to-side movement) and have minimal dorso- ventral (up and down) movement. Distinct white stripe down arms. Arm spines, up to ten, usually long (six times arm segment length), glassy, more or less serrated and tapering, lower spines short and often just stumps. Species very active on deck, readily flipping from dorsal to ventral sides. Frequently associated with sponges.

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: Disc usually darker than arms, colours vary from orange, grey, red to pink. Arms with light white longitudinal line, sometimes with pink or red stripes bordering the line.

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: Disc diameter up to 16 mm. Arms long, nine times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Sodwana Bay; usually more than 200 m depth.

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: Ophiothrix fragilis, which has shorter arms, spines on radial shields and does not have the distinctive white stripe along arms.

title: Ophiothrix aristulata
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). pp. 142-143. (277pp.). Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. PhD dissertation, Department of Biological Sciences, University of Cape Town, South Africa. pp. 284-285. (434pp.).

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiothrichidae; genus: Ophiothrix; species: fragilis; common name: Bristly brittle star

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: Dorsal disc covered in thorny spinelets, stumps and spines; may be intermixed. Radial shields large, covered with spines. Arm spines up to ten, glassy, thorny over total length, not tapering, sometimes lowermost spine transformed into a hook, longest spine not more than three times segment length. Long spines protrude along the margins of the length of the arms, giving a ‘feathery’ appearance. Tips of the arms are readily discarded when disturbed. Shallow, abundant species.

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: Orange to red, often with darker brown, grey or purple central disc. May have various combinations of oranges, reds, greens, greys, browns, purples, yellows and pinks. Arms banded and often with dots associated with dorsal arm plates longitudinally along arms.

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: Disc diameter up to 20 mm. Arms moderate in length, three to five times disc diameter.

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Kosi Bay; less than 100 m depth.

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: Ophiothrix abyssicola and O. aristulata, which have longer arms and naked radial shields while O. fragilis has spines on radial shields and shorter arms.

title: Ophiothrix fragilis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). pp. 144-145. (277pp.). Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. PhD dissertation, Department of Biological Sciences, University of Cape Town, South Africa. pp. 288-290. (434pp.).

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiomyxidae; genus: Ophiolycus; species: dentatus; common name: Toothed brittle star

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: Disc pentagonal, covered in thick skin. Radial shields narrow, just shorter than width of arm base, not distinct. Oral papillae spiniform, long. Teeth similar in shape, but smaller and clustered at apex of jaw. Arms five, simple, length moderate. Dorsal arm plates fragmented especially basally, covered by thick skin. Arm spines three, lowermost cigar- shaped, broad and flattened, approximately one segment length, remaining spines spiniform, uppermost being slightly longer than segment length, distal spines becoming hook-shaped. Often damaged in sample.

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: Red to orange dorsally, lighter ventrally. Colouration sometimes fades to white from trawl damage. Arms red, mottled.

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms three times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: Southern African endemic. West Coast (Groen river) to East Coast (Sodwana Bay) of South Africa; 129-450 m depth.

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis is glossier in appearance and Ophiolycus dentatus has larger, more obvious arm spines and many spine-shaped oral papillae.

title: Ophiolycus dentatus
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). p. 135. (277pp.). Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. PhD dissertation, Department of Biological Sciences, University of Cape Town, South Africa. pp. 160-162. (434pp.).

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiuridae; genus: Ophiomyxa; species: vivipara capensis; common name: Bright red disc brittle star

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: Bright red/orange in colour. Disc pentagonal, covered with thick, smooth, glossy skin. Radial shields short, but not distinct in fresh specimens. Oral papillae three to four, broad, serrated, flattened, with transparent edges. Teeth similar, four to five. Arms five, moderately long, flexible and tapered, mottled in colouration, also covered in thick skin. Arm spines slender, serrated and rugose at tip, up to four on free segments. Disintegrates quickly out of water and is often severely damaged in trawls.

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: Bright glossy red, yellow or orange disc, mottled red/ orange/white arms.

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms three to four times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: Endemic. West Coast off Orange River to East Coast, East London; 101-450 m depth.

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: Ophiolycus dentatus, but Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis has a smoother appearance, arm spines are shorter (not obvious) and thorny but often covered in skin. Teeth flat and glassy.

title: Ophiomyxa vivipara capensis
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). pp. 134-135. (277pp.). Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. PhD dissertation, Department of Biological Sciences, University of Cape Town, South Africa. pp. 159-160. (434pp.).

title: Ophiocten affinis simulans
content: Small species. Disc scales large circular plates, all encircled by smaller scales. Radial shields separated by scales. Edge of disc slightly indented at arms. Arm combs present. Oral papillae three each side of apical papillae, distalmost broad. Three slender and pointed arm spines.

title: Ophiocten affinis simulans
content: Light brown to grey.

title: Ophiocten affinis simulans
content: Disc diameter up to 4 mm. Arms three times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiocten affinis simulans
content: Endemic. West Coast, off Lamberts Bay to South Coast, Port Alfred; depth range 55-273 m.

content: None.

title: Ophiomisidium pulchellum
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiuridae; genus: Ophiomisidium; species: pulchellum; common name: Spiky orange brittle star

title: Ophiomisidium pulchellum
content: Very small species, seldom encountered. Disc round, disc scales large, thick and taking up most of dorsal disc. Radial shields oval. Oral papillae two, fused each side of triangular apical papillae. Arms rigid, short, consisting of approximately 15 segments only. Spiky in appearance due to spines on arms and disc. Arm spines three, enlarged, flattened, blunt, and rapidly decreasing in size down arm.

title: Ophiomisidium pulchellum
content: Pale orange.

title: Ophiomisidium pulchellum
content: Disc diameter up to 5 mm. Arms one to two times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiomisidium pulchellum
content: West Coast, off Cape Town to East Coast, south of Durban; 70-3 065 m depth.

title: Ophiomisidium pulchellum
content: None.

title: Ophiura trimeni
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiuridae; genus: Ophiura (Ophiura); species: trimeni; common name: Orange stripe brittle star

title: Ophiura trimeni
content: Disc scales covered in thin skin. Radial shields half disc radius, twice as long as wide, not touching. Mouth or oral slit usually wide open, oral papillae three, distalmost broadest, apical papillae pointed. Teeth three to five, same shape as apical papillae. Arm spines three, spines twice segment length, one segment length towards end of arms. Orange and white longitudinal striped arms. Patterned disc with orange and white shapes. Very small, fragile species. Very common and abundant.

title: Ophiura trimeni
content: Orange and white.

title: Ophiura trimeni
content: Disc diameter up to 9 mm. Arms three to four times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiura trimeni
content: Endemic. West Coast, off Orange River to East Coast, Sodwana Bay; 165-1 647 m depth.

title: Ophiura trimeni
content: None.

title: Ophiura costata costata
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiuridae; genus: Ophiura (Ophiuroglypha); species: costata costata; common name: rigid orange brittle star

title: Ophiura costata costata
content: Arms and disc inflexible (rigid), arms often broken. Disc pentagonal, disc scales distinct, thick, irregular, forming star shape on disc edged in darker orange colour. Radial shields longer than wide, oval, separated by scales. Mouth narrow or tightly closed. Arms fairly long when unbroken, can be more than four times disc diameter. Arm spines three, very short and appressed to arm.

title: Ophiura costata costata
content: Orange to orange-red.

title: Ophiura costata costata
content: Disc diameter up to 23 mm. Arms often broken, but can be more than four times disc diameter.

title: Ophiura costata costata
content: Endemic. West Coast, off Orange River to South Coast, Cape St Francis; 43-1 647 m depth.

title: Ophiura costata costata

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiuridae; genus: Ophiactis; species: abyssicola; common name: Abyss brittle star

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: Disc round, sparsely scattered conical spines on disc, concentrated on margin. Radial shields naked, oblong to rectangular. Arms five, simple, long, moniliform (like string of beads) distally. Three to four arm spines, erect, may be pointed or blunt, cylindrical, middle spine longest, half to two times longer than segment.

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: Orange arms with darker purple, grey or brown disc; some specimens with a pinkish tinge.

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: Disc diameter up to 8 mm. Arms three to eight times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: West Coast, off Cape Columbine to South Coast off Still Bay; 167-2 743 m depth.

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: Ophiothrix fragilis, Ophiothrix aristulata and Ophiactis carnea, but Ophiactis abyssicola is distinguished by conical spines on disc and naked radial shields.

title: Ophiactis abyssicola
content: Clark AM and Courtman-Stock J. 1976. The Echinoderms of Southern Africa. London, British Museum (Natural History). p. 161. (277pp.). Clark HL. 1923. The echinoderm fauna of South Africa. Annals of the South African Museum 13(7): 221-438. pp. 232-233. Olbers JM. 2016. Taxonomy, Biodiversity and Biogeography of the Ophiuroidea of South Africa. title: Ophiactis carnea
content: Arms five, simple. Disc round, covered in spines, sometimes with darkened area or blotch in centre of disc visible. Radial shields naked, elongated D-shaped, moderate in size. Three to five arm spines.

title: Ophiactis carnea
content: Reddish brown to pink, brown or orange, sometimes with white patches.

title: Ophiactis carnea
content: Disc diameter up to 6 mm. Arms five to six times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiactis carnea
content: West Coast, beyond Lambert’s Bay, off Cape Town to East Coast, Cape St Lucia; intertidal to 220 m depth.

title: Ophiactis carnea
content: Ophiothrix fragilis and Ophiactis abyssicola, but Ophiactis carnea has D-shaped radial shields.

title: Ophiomitrella hamata
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Ophiuroidea; order: Ophiurida; family: Ophiuridae; genus: Ophiomitrella; species: hamata; common name: coal stack brittle star

title: Ophiomitrella hamata
content: Very small species, disc round and covered with short blunt stumps. Radial shields oval in shape, short. Five arms, usually curled under disc or attached to coral or sea fan. Five arm spines, longest not exceeding segment length.

title: Ophiomitrella hamata
content: Light purple or white.

title: Ophiomitrella hamata
content: Disc diameter up to 4 mm. Arms three times disc diameter in length.

title: Ophiomitrella hamata
content: Endemic. South Coast, off Mossel Bay to East Coast, Durban; 63-900 m depth.

title: Ophiomitrella hamata
content: None known, although may be confused with Astrothorax waitei which also attach to sea fans and other biogenic species.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Holothuroidea; order: Dendrochirotida; family: Thyonidae; genus: Thyone; species: venusta; common name: Orange and white speckled sea cucumber

title: Thyone venusta
common_name: Orange and white speckled sea cucumber
content: U-shaped body, cylindrical, with posterior end turned upward. Skin smooth, but appears ‘hairy’ due to numerous scattered fine tube feet (podia). Speckled orange and white colour, darker dorsally.

title: Thyone venusta
common_name: Orange and white speckled sea cucumber
content: White, speckled with orange.

common_name: Orange and white speckled sea cucumber
content: 90-100 mm in length, width 8-10 mm.

title: Thyone venusta

content: Juvenile Thyone aurea on West Coast, which are more uniform orange/pink in colour and not U-shaped.

content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Holothuroidea; order: Dendrochirotida; family: Cucumariidae; genus: Hemiocnus; species: insolens; common name: red-chested sea cucumber

title: Hemiocnus insolens
content: Small, solid sea cucumber distinguished by its bright colours red or yellow, although white variations are also common, especially on the West Coast. Solid, slightly gelatinous texture. Tube feet scattered all round. Ten irregularly branched tentacles. Usually occurs in dense colonies, especially on the West Coast.

title: Hemiocnus insolens
content: Usually bright red, yellow or white, but can vary.

title: Hemiocnus insolens
content: 25-60 mm in length.

title: Hemiocnus insolens
content: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa as far east as Port Elizabeth. Intertidal to 110 m.

title: Hemiocnus insolens
content: Pseudocnella sykion and P. sinorbis in shallow intertidal waters.

title: Hemiocnus insolens

title: Psolus griffithsi
content: Distinct species identifiable by the dorsal scales covering the body and the sucker-like ventral surface forming a sole. Scales overlapping and covered with minute granules. Tentacles are bushy when visible. Tube feet (podia) present on ventral sole in two rows; outer row minute and inner row much larger.

title: Psolus griffithsi
content: Beige scales with orange/brown centres, ventral sole grey to brown.

title: Psolus griffithsi
content: 20-25 mm length.

title: Psolus griffithsi
content: Endemic. West Coast of South Africa.

title: Psolus griffithsi
content: Psolus agulhasicus.

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: phylum: Echinodermata; class: Holothuroidea; order: Aspidochirotida; family: Synallactidae; genus: Pseudostichopus; species: langeae; common name: Sand covered sea cucumber

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: Cylindrical body form with ventral surface slightly flattened and dorsal surface slightly arched. Thick, leathery and smooth body wall, usually encrusted with sand grains, broken shells, coral debris, echinoid spines and foraminifera, but no pteropod shells or sponge spicules. Tiny tube feet (podia) mostly along dorso-lateral edges. Retains firm shape out of water. Mouth located on ventral surface with between 18 and 20 peltate (leaf- or shield-shaped) projecting tentacles, cream to brown in colour. Anus located sub-ventrally in a distinct pygal (posterior) furrow.

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: Skin is covered in sand grains, but when the encrustations are washed off, the skin is opaque, off-white to cream in colour.

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: Up to 70 mm in length, 8-10 mm diameter.

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: Endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, ranging in depth from ± 100-400 m.

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: Pseudostichopus echinatus from the East Coast.

title: Pseudostichopus langeae
content: Thandar AS. 2009. New species and a new record of sea cucumbers from deep waters of the South African temperate region (Echinodermata: Holothuroidea). Zootaxa 2013: 30–42. Species identification by Ahmed Thandar.

title: Zygothuria lactea
content: Very slimy, soft body wall with folded outer skin that readily disintegrates off main body. Has 20 pink to orange-coloured tentacles visible at mouth. Tube feet greatly reduced and difficult to detect.

title: Zygothuria lactea
content: Light brown to mud-coloured outer skin layer, with pale pink to white body wall.

title: Zygothuria lactea
content: Up to 140 mm in length.

title: Zygothuria lactea
content: Deeper waters – three individual specimens captured at 369, 617 and 907 m on West coast of South Africa.

title: Synallactes mollis
content: Gelatinous, slimy body wall with thin outer brown skin layer (frequently torn) covering pale purple body wall beneath. Maintains shape on trawl deck but not rigid. Tube feet variable in size, decreasing in size posteriorly. A double ring of 16 to 22 tentacles present.

title: Synallactes mollis
content: Brown outer skin to purple body wall with darker tube feet.

title: Synallactes mollis
content: Up to 120-185 mm in length.

title: Synallactes mollis
content: Endemic. South Coast of South Africa.

title: Synallactes mollis

title: Synallactes viridilimus
content: Large gelatinous body, often slimy. Thin body wall. Mouth with 20 peltate (leaf- or shield-shaped) crown of tentacles, orange to yellow in colour. Upper tentacles in single row, lower tentacles in double row. Ventro-lateral tube feet (podia) more prominent and longer than mid-ventral tube feet.

title: Synallactes viridilimus
content: Brown to pale purple in colour. Tube feet darker purple.

title: Synallactes viridilimus
content: Up to 450 mm in length.

title: Synallactes viridilimus
content: Endemic. West Coast of South Africa.

content: Synallactes mollis is smaller in size and usually occurs on the South Coast.

title: Synallactes sp.
content: Large gelatinous body wall coated in substantial slime that is readily rubbed off along with body wall tissue. Retains shape out of water, but body wall tissue not very robust to handling and is easily damaged. Only one specimen recorded to date.

title: Synallactes sp.
content: Pale purple/lilac colour with darker oral and anal areas.

title: Ascidia incrassata
content: phylum: Chordata; subphylum: Tunicata; class: Ascidiacea (sea squirts); order: Phlebobranchia; family: Ascidiidae; genus: Ascidia; species: incrassata; common name: Orange sea squirt

title: Ascidia incrassata
content: Characteristic red bands between each of the eight branchial siphon lobes. Colouration of lobes may be obscured when the siphons are closed and retracted. Both siphons, situated on the anterior part of the elongated globular body, may be covered in varying degrees of mud and sand. Body colour varies from red to orange and yellow. If no bands occur, photograph and retain specimen.

title: Ascidia incrassata
content: Orange to red or even purple tunic (outer body wall), with red bands on inside of siphon.

title: Ascidia incrassata
content: Up to 140 mm.

title: Ascidia incrassata
content: Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa to Mozambique. Recorded as invasive off the Pacific Coast of Panama. Intertidally to a depth of 114 m.

title: Ascidia incrassata
content: Pyura stolonifera (red bait) is yellow brown and has a tough, leathery tunic but no red bands on siphons.

title: Ascidia incrassata
